item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
After two years thinking about this, I'm still under most of the same impressions. I think it deserves a good rewrite, and easier to do as it seems, to work better. It is too low on votes for such a good concept.

1- OoC People won't get educated if they can't understand or care about the subject matter, IC people need to have an understanding of the article once they have clearance because it is relevant to a task they are there to accomplish. Not all people reading an article is a researcher, and not always on the same subjects.

SOLUTION: A heavier use of footnotes could work wonders, here. The idea isn't to get rid from technical stuff per se, but to make the article easier to digest. A rewrite with simpler language can still keep the technical stuff on footnotes. Most of the original text would remain unchanged but relocated.

2- Get rid of the photos, already! Since those are human tissues, just get some photos of human tissue. If you want, you can ask someone here to modify those photos to add explorators or exploration equipment. This article don't need the caves, though.

3- A log of uncommon enlarged organisms found on specific occasions would be far better than the distinct entry into E. Colli as a separate SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Bloated, overly dense and complicated to navigate and understand, I found myself frequently going back and forth trying to see if you had already explained something you mentioned or getting confused by the -1 -2 -3 -4 -a -b -c rainbow triangle subwoofer or whatever. Not amusing.

It is with a heavy heart that I downvote this piece because you worked a lot on it and it shows! But I just can't enjoy it as it is.

item.title: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Okay, so, both SCP-2431 and SCP-2946 are said to have been discovered during the exploration of SCP-2378, so it is likely that the SCPF would mention that in their documentation. Additionally, with such devices as SCP-3536 available, it's likely that the SCPF may use them in the testing of Tissue Dome's contents. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Adding the following to the end of the Containment Procedures:

Use of SCP-3536-1 instances have been approved for testing within SCP-2378 on a case by case basis.

Changing this:

Further exploration lead to the discovery of attached anatomical structures, as detailed in the previous sections (See exploration logs E-2378-1 through 4). On August 5th, 1997, SCP-2378 underwent its first observed transition period 3 months after genetic identification. 2 teams were within the structure during the transition, resulting in █ personnel becoming trapped in dense tissue, and loss of GPS contact with ██ personnel.

To this:

Further exploration lead to the discovery of attached anatomical structures, as well as SCP-2431 and SCP-2946, as detailed in the previous sections (See exploration logs E-2378-1 through 4). On August 5th, 1997, SCP-2378 underwent its first observed transition period 3 months after genetic identification. 2 teams were within the structure during the transition, resulting in █ personnel becoming trapped in dense tissue, and loss of GPS contact with ██ personnel.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It isn't a good reason for a new SCP designation. Distinct SCPs are so because their Containment Procedures are unrelated. As interesting as the giant pathogens and other "non-native" cells are, they have pretty standard CPs after what is linked to 2378.

You could instead create a log of entries describing each of those cells (or only the ones you want, actually) as an "attached document", instead of another SCP. The site already offers several formats to explore things outside the standard SCP page.

About the photos, maybe it is feasible to photoshop some large things, like people, into an image of those organs, or make an image that transits from organ to cave.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's true that the precedent is to have object from within an anomaly be designated -#, however here is a bit of a different case. Because the Object is spatially linked to a real world person, the organisms within tend to vary based on the linked host. If an entity of interest appears, Foundation teams will extract it and designate it as it's own SCP because, when the structure changes links, the entity may vanish altogether.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While the execution and concept is interesting, I find it strange that the foundation would designate pathogens inside the SCP as individual SCPs. In the case of SCP-354, the entities that emerged from the SCP is designated as SCP-354-#. Other then that, its perfect.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Indeed, I have plans to expand them, I'm in the planning stages, but as of right now I'm still working through how to write them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I quite like it, despite the rather clunky grammar and wording in some parts. Remember, technical documents are clipped and mechanical, so long sentences should usually be avoided. Other than that, I thought it was detailed and well-fleshed out (pun intended). It's certainly nice to see an SCP that manages to tell an interesting story without it being "impending XK #3,297." Will you be expanding on the exploration logs in the future?

item.title: Re: Upvote, but with nitpicks
item.content:encoded: Very true, I appreciate the feedback though

item.title: Re: Upvote, but with nitpicks
item.content:encoded:
Regarding the photos, I really do feel that you would be better off without them, or at least with some reasonable replacement.

Regarding the -4 issue, it's not so much that I'm proposing a rewrite, as that I wanted to give advice for "next time" for you. You might have done better if you had not bothered with building suspense about the -4s. But you might have done even better if you proposed some kind of "-5" that corresponds to no known cell in the human body (and whether it corresponds to a somehow-undiscovered cell type is [REDACTED]).

It's just inherent in the Lovecraftian influences on this site, I think, and maybe also in human nature. Gesturing at something sinister, something just off-screen, or simply inscrutable, works better than spelling out a realistic danger.

item.title: Re: Upvote, but with nitpicks
item.content:encoded:
Perhaps I should remove the photos then, I attempted to find photos that better matched the descriptions, however the initial feedback I got when I used photos resembling the tissues was that just looked like human organs, and weren't really stacking up…looking back based on the feedback here, that person gave me really bad feedback for what I was aiming for.

The -4 coming so late criticism is valid, although at this point I would basically need to rewrite portions of the entire article, and I'm not sure that will do much for the people who have already upvoted/downvoted.

That's part of why I write like I do, to encourage people to look up stuff and educate themselves on what's really going on.

item.title: Upvote, but with nitpicks
item.content:encoded:
I loved this overall, but I had problems with two elements (both mentioned by other commenters as well).

1: The photos seem shoehorned in. They don't look like organic structures, or like any crystallized sugars I've seen. I don't feel like they accomplished anything except to distract me by failing to match the SCP.

2: The "-4" are described far too late. If the containment procedures say to avoid something, that thing should be described in the containment procedures themselves, or in the main body of the description. As things are, the -4's are built up a bit throughout the article, but there's no "climax". Maybe I've just read this site too long, but things-what-explode-folk aren't too exciting to me. It would be better to just explain them earlier, and focus on other things rather than hint at the -4's.

I actually really like the technical detail, though. SCP's aren't scientific papers, but it's something of a given that people who examine the description in detail are Foundation researchers. Maybe there could be some rearrangement to make the details clearer to laymen, but I personally don't mind having to look some things up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The plan is for those logs to get made, I've just had trouble really nailing down where to start with it.

item.title: Not yet an upvote, but soon
item.content:encoded:
This is really, really good. But I have 4 very streamlined issues with the skip:

1- Too technical in the wrong way: I could follow most of the description, but I disagree that the language is a part of the skip. It isn't half-bad, and certainly I'm not proposing to make it informal. But the main section of a skip is meant for focus on the anomaly, not on explaining science. It would be better understood both for actual readers and for fictional agents that need the entry, if most of this was in a language accessible to lay personnel and with focus on consequences and anomalous.

Most of the time, too technical material would be deemed for a vaguely described addenda needed for researchers who need those details, instead making the main entry too hard to read.

2- SCP-2946 and others shouldn't be SCPs: SCP isn't the anomaly, it is the Special Containment Procedure. As much as the germs inside 2378 are separate entities, they are attached to the same Procedure. That's what the -X are for. You'll see that in a lot of skips the -Xs are completely distinct entities or phenomena, sometimes only tangentially related. But what makes them a collective bound to one SCP is that the procedure for containment itself is related.

To keep SCP-2946 as an entry, you can link as a supplemental addenda for description of the life and viral forms found inside 2378.

3- Not the best choices in photos: seriously, the description simply don't match. The caverns resemble loosely the described structures, but as "inside photos" they aren't working for me.

4- Last logs: do them, or drop them. You can easily refer to such documents existing in the test without making "blocked entries" for them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My only beef with this is the utterly pointless last addenda. If none of those logs are ever going to be made I would cut it entirely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would suggest going through and adding to each technical explanation to every structure and cell type exactly what human cell the anomalous cell is analogous to; The foundation would favor documentation that is clear and concise, and stating immediately that one structure is an erythrocyte as opposed to a lymphocyte provides a lot of information immediately to a qualified reader as to the functions and potential behaviors of the anomaly, which would be vital if there was an emergency that someone must immediately handle.

Other than needing immediate clarity in the beginning, the technical portions are both interesting, strange and good!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! =)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Part of the appeal of the SCP is that it defies normal biophysical evolution, including the proportions of different interior elements. The large and Small intestines are incredibly different in terms of width at varying positions within each structure, thus at different points in the structure you'll see different widths.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hi Pig, It's the collapsible Addendum 2, I'll fix it so it's more visible!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wasn't able to find the part where it explains what -4 is. Halp?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2378-A are a collection of intertwining caverns and tunnels, 50-70 km in length and 100m to 1km in width.

I don't know anything about biology but that width seems entirely out of proportion to everything else. My first thought on reading that was to compare it to the outside, which is only 3km across. But maybe I'm just wrong?

I also feel that there are a ton of bio references that are completely lost on me. That makes me sad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very wordy and technical, but hey, that's the whole style of this place - wordy and technical. For a second SCP, very well-researched and executed. I couldn't read the whole thing because my attention span is about as long as a gnat's dick, but what I could read I was very impressed by. a +1 for you, good sir. Good to see SCPs of this quality are still being made.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoyed the SCP, but I do have one minor gripe.
The photos look like you googled stock images of a cave fitting your description and put them on there. It's very minor, and I'm not the best, so take what I say with a grain of salt.

Great SCP though! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mysterious, surprising, mixed with a bit of SCIENCE. I love it.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I appreciate your feedback. Macro-biology and anatomy are, frankly, not my forte. I specialize in microbiology, so my knowledge of anatomical systems and tissues is lacking in some respect. I can see navigating some of the sentence rearrangements, and will probably do those, as well as the note about blood color. In regards to your point about the sugar sensing, I was being as nonspecific as possible to avoid stumbling into jargon territory. As far as what happens to the Human body, considering glucose is the primary energy source for us, I'll let you imagine what happens there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nitpicking
I have hawk eyes for weird phrasing and science

Just a forewarning some of this is just like, my opinion, man. I tend to phrase things in very deliberate ways when writing to help the flow, so feel free to disregard semantics-related bits unless you wanna go for it.

Personnel are to avoid physical interaction with SCP-2378-4 iterations at all costs.

I would rearrange this to be "Physical interaction with SCP-2378-4 should(must?) be avoided by personnel at all costs."

Entry into SCP-2378-D is restricted to aerial corrosion-proof drones.

What kind of material would be needed for this? Maybe put a footnote with recommended materials.

Personnel should avoid irritation of interior elements of SCP-2378.

Another bit I'd rearrange; "Irritation to the interior element of SCP-2378 should be avoided."

Each of these organisms is capable of sensing large concentrations of sugar, even in a human host, and will engage in aggressive predatory behavior to secure these sources.

This sentence is definitely kind of wonky to me. Maybe "These organisms have specialized sensory organelles for detecting significant accumulations of sugars (all sugar? or just glucose? or what?). They have predated on humans to collect the sugar content within them." Although the last bit of mine is also pretty weird. Maybe footnote it? Also, what do they do with the rest of the human body bits?

viscous maroon or dark-blue/purple colored fluid compositionally similar to human plasma.

Human plasma is actually a light amber colour! And if you mean deoxygenated blood, it only really ever gets to a dark maroon colour. The cells of veins appear blue because they are, but the blood within is still primarily red. Here's a visual example; the darker sample is deoxygenated.

I also personally prefer footnotes to be placed after punctuation.1

I have hawk eyes for weird phrasing and science

edit: accidental footnote removed :p

Overall: I love biology horror. I think the actual biology-related stuff could use some tightening up in general. Bio is one of my best subjects for science though, so might not be fair to those not great at it, so if the simplification is keeping the learning curve down, that's fine.

+1!

Footnotes
1. Like this; it looks neater to me, but meh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's no actual size limit, more of a general rule to not post gigantic images that take several seconds to load.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry about the image size, I went back to look at the image use policy to see what the limitations were, but couldn't seem to find the size limit again. Probably cause I'm frazzled from lack of sleep. I went ahead downloaded and reuploaded it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The third image is WAY too big, at 12mb. I've shrunken down the image— if you could download it here and replace the article image with this version, since I can't delete the files here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I can see why this can leave others bogged down in the technical jargon and length, as someone with a degree in the bio-sciences this was both incredibly fascinating and a good use of body horror, especially the effects of exposure to SCP-2378-4. Additionally, the use of photos here really enhanced the article for me, as it made visualizing these various pathways easier, and helped bring the article to life.

+1. Good show.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This SCP is both a medium for an idea I had revolving around a spatially linked biological anomaly, and will serve as sort of a…hub for micro themed articles of sorts. Of course I'm not going to saturate the wiki with new Micro SCP's every week, nor am I going to be repetitive with the descriptions. SCPs attached to this…sub series I guess is what I will call it will be more focused on the individual parts and stories that each of these things have to convey about the world building I am beginning through these articles so to speak. Technical writing is where I excel, so I'm challenging myself to weave an interconnected series of entities that are self contained in their own stories, but play into a larger narrative that I'm just beginning to plan.

Sorry, there was a lot of babbling in there. Basically, are the articles connected? Yes. Will there be an ever expanding list? Probably not. What you see in the article are a variety of Ideas that have popped into my head, and that I think I might be able to develop into separate articles. Some of them will probably never be written, purely because they fall too closely to what I already did with 2946. Others are so bizarrely different that it would be a crime for me not to write an article about them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Normally I don't like overly technical articles when it comes to biology, as that's one of the scientists I care the least about. But despite that I stayed for the whole ride and ended up enjoying it, so +1.

I did notice that this article seemed to hint at a large amount of related SCPs similar to 2946, is this going to be an ever expanding list? Or is it just a convenience or something to link the two?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You have no idea how much this pun tempted me to change the title.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So it's a

man cave

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll temper the above and state that I do respect the amount of technical knowledge in this piece and the one before. As I said, it's a matter of personal taste on my part, really, and I don't want to actively discourage something on the sole basis of my not being into it. Also, with your response taken into account, I don't want to give the impression that this is some sort of cynical exercise. Clearly there's a base of readers who enjoy this style, so more power to you on that count.

I look forward to seeing your continued work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I appreciate your feedback Kalinin, and want to lay your fears to rest in regards to "A formula being milked". I am moving away from the "Giant microbe is giant and defies normal biophysical concepts" and focusing more on the story the SCP has to tell, and what impacts their individual anomalous effects have. I focused more heavily on the square cube law and technical aspects in this and the previous article primarily because that's what I'm used to writing, and to establish myself on site with such high standards it became apparent that I would need to stick to my strengths and work on developing my narrative ability within clinical tone. From this point moving forward I will be attempting to balance narrative against detail, in a different manner from what you've seen so far to avoid the repetition and similarity you see between the two current pieces.

It's unfortunate that you mention the Prion article, as it's pretty clear from the writing that that particular piece was trying to play off the relative success of 2946. I critiqued that piece before it was posted, and attempted to guide the writer into a different direction from where I had tread, as well as correct his incorrect scientific assumptions. It's even more unfortunate that he only half listened to my critique, and left many of the issues I first pointed out unattended, while adding an exacerbated experiment log that was verbatim a direct copy of 2946's format.

Again, I reassure you that this won't become a formula from which to milk upvotes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wasn't really enamored of "microscopic structures but larger" as a trick the first time it was deployed, and seeing it again so soon isn't really helping that.

I held off on commenting on the first iteration of this, because while I don't really like articles that lean heavily on technical precepts and things like violating the Square Cube Law, I can see that there's probably a place for that sort of thing. But seeing the same general concept, executed in largely the same way, now we're at a point where it feels like a formula being milked for all its worth, and I'd rather not see repeated exercises in the same thing.

I mean, it's refreshing to see articles that don't get their length from overly narmy interview logs or fifteen collapsibles about how the real document is in the next castle, but between these two articles and the prion one earlier this week, this is a trend that I don't really want to see either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is body horror in Cinemascope. The breadth of scale combined with the carefully observed details really made this work for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I appreciate the feed back, in regards to B and D, I thought I had included statements correlating to what you've said. Guess I didn't, I'll see if I can fix the wording there to indicate as much. As far as the Technical Jargon goes, it continues to surprise me what is and is not considered Jargon, which varies based from person to person. The text is a bit dense and long, I agree, but as far as Jargon, in comparison to the the previous drafts, it is quite trimmed.

So the linked individual being a foundation employee was, honestly, on my part completely intentional, same goes for the discovery. There's no clear indicator as to why it happens like this in-universe, I thought about making a more clear connection, however after some debate I decided it would be better to leave the reader thinking about what might be really going on here, if whatever is behind the anomaly knows about the foundation, and is guiding them in some manner.

It's also important to remember that full sequencing of the human genome didn't occur until 2003, however since foundation techniques and technology is usually at least a decade ahead of the general public they were able to employ it early. An increase in Class 1 events lead to concern that something might happen, and so they employed this novel technique to try and see if something was happening on a genetic level. What did they find? That this thing was genetically identical to one of their operatives, an operative, who, worked around the entity and was recently diagnosed with Colon cancer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are a lot of parts to this and I think the density of the technical jargon takes away from an average reader's ability to understand article, but I think I was able to muddle through enough to give this a +1. All the technical detail does feel like the crux of the article, after all. It just feels a bit like a chore to get through in places. Also I'm a little surprised the documentation doesn't explicitly say that -B areas are like human blood vessels and -D areas are like human stomachs.

Another thing that stuck out to me is that this anomaly was apparently first discovered by the Foundation, while they were looking for somewhere to build their research site for microbiological anomalies, and the first known linked individual was a Foundation employee. That's… one hell of a coincidence (provided there isn't some cleverly-hinted reason as to why this might be that I missed). Still, this is fiction, so my +1 stays. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Big, ambitious, weird, super-technical - and ultimately stellarly executed. +1. Excellent work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey everyone, so this is my second SCP, it's rather lengthy but I think you'll find the detail and implications behind it to satisfy it's length. Many thanks to Weryllium, DrSolo, sinns, Westydude, and inithello for proofreading and vetting the drafts.

Images are covered under creative commons and originate from wiki visually and wikiwand. http://wikivisually.com/wiki/Lava_tube and http://www.wikiwand.com/en/Karst.
